verdict: Reading passes all checks in cycle 2. Word count ~930 (sidecar-reported); 930 / 230 wpm = 4.04 min, which clears the 4.0 min floor and falls within the [4, 5] min target. The ~60-word expansion to the else-clause explanation (line 84) — adding the boundary-clarity rationale — resolves the cycle 1 hard fail. Format is clean: no frontmatter, correct H1, sidecar present and parseable, six sections in canonical order. All three sources cited inline with no orphans. No forward references beyond two compliant named-and-deferred pointers (finally → later topic; Anthropic API → topic 12.12). All 10 sampled assertions trace to the corpus. Every non-everyday term is defined on first use with bold-term / plain-meaning shape. No wall-of-text, no unlisted sequences, no assessment references, no scope creep. One soft note: the sidecar field reading_minutes is reported as 5, which implies ~186 wpm rather than the mandated 230 wpm formula; the correct computed value is 4.0. This is a sidecar metadata inconsistency only and does not gate approval, but the sidecar should be corrected to reading_minutes: 4.0 before close.

format.notes: First non-empty line is the correct H1 title. Sidecar parses cleanly with all required fields. All six mandatory sections present in canonical order with no additions, renames, or drops.

faithfulness.notes: All 10 sampled assertions supported by corpus: (1) crash = immediate stop on unhandled exception (corpus §4); (2) exception = runtime signal (corpus §5.1); (3) syntax error vs runtime distinction (corpus §5.1); (4) try wraps risky code, except runs on failure (corpus §5.2–5.3); (5) bare except catches typos, keyboard interrupts (corpus §5.4); (6) else = happy path, runs when no exception (corpus §5.5); (7) else code not protected by except above (corpus §5.5); (8) read_marks() returns [] on FileNotFoundError (corpus §5.6); (9) silent swallow anti-pattern (corpus §8); (10) finally as one deferred note (corpus §8 final note).

beginner_readability.notes: All non-everyday terms defined on first use with bold-term / plain-meaning shape: exception (line 11), raises (line 11), syntax errors (line 13), runtime (line 13), try block (line 30), except block (line 30), bare except (line 49), else block (line 72). Concrete-first ordering maintained throughout — file-crash scenario precedes the term 'exception handling'. No hedging language. Average sentence length well under 25 words.

formatting.notes: Longest prose paragraph (line 84, else-clause expansion) is 3 sentences — within the 6-sentence ceiling. Step-by-step sequences rendered as numbered lists (lines 41–45). In Practice uses bullet lists. Inline code examples appear throughout Key Concepts (e.g., int('hello') for ValueError, bare-except snippet). Bold-term / plain-meaning shape used consistently for definitions.
